data.title: United Airlines Can Weather This Storm - Earnings Preview (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The U.S. airline industry came out of the post covid gate running as soon as demand began to return in 2022. United Airlines (NASDAQ:UAL) made the decision during covid to not retire any aircraft and to manage its staffing so that it could take advantage of demand when it returned. Although each of American (AAL), Delta (DAL) and Southwest (LUV) have larger domestic route systems than UAL and domestic travel returned before international travel post-covid, United wanted to make sure it was well-positioned to grow its international network. Pre-covid, Delta was the largest carrier across the Atlantic based on capacity but United overtook Delta post-covid. United has been the largest airline across the Pacific, regardless of nationality, for about 10 years. Since Asian travel was slower to restart than within the U.S. or in Latin America or Europe, United was well-positioned to grow its transpacific presence particularly against Asian carriers. It set out bold plans to accelerate its growth via massive aircraft orders and airport terminal expansions. United’s margins began to rise closer to Delta which has been the U.S. market leader in revenue, profits, and market capitalization. I identified a number of risks in this Seeking Alpha article just two months ago expressing my fears that UAL’s stock ascent could not continue. The entire stock market has faced enormous turbulence since the beginning of the year with UAL losing one-third of its value so it is worth re-evaluating my rating on UAL.

Industry Context

Delta released its first quarter earnings on Wednesday April 9. DAL is typically the first major airline in the world and one of the first large U.S. consumer companies to report so its report always attracts attention. Its earnings were generally in line with its previously revised guidance but it pulled its full year guidance based on uncertainty esp. around trade and tariffs. It noted weakness in the domestic coach market and says that it is fairly comfortable with international and domestic demand through the second quarter which heavily includes outbound travel by Americans to Europe but is less certain about the demand profile after the middle of August when many American schools reopen for the new school year. It plans to reduce capacity in the second quarter with an eye to even further reductions in the second half of the year.

In light of Delta’s commentary, all eyes will be on United’s guidance for the second quarter and its potential commentary and guidance for the second quarter and beyond. Based on my expectations and considering expected airline industry performance, I will evaluate United’s previously outlined plans and strategies which are generally more growth and expansion oriented than Delta’s.

In considering the industry backdrop, it is important to note that UAL’s financial position relative to the industry has improved during the post-covid period. Premium and international travel has provided considerable strength to Delta and United who lead the industry in those segments. Domestic leisure travel has been under pressure since the earliest days of the covid recovery. U.S. airlines were heavily subsidized during covid through government grants that required airlines to retain employees in return for covering their salary and benefits costs. There has been overcapacity in the domestic sector since covid. Inflation has weakened buying power esp. for lower income consumers. Airline labor costs have increased faster than capacity, resulting in deteriorating labor cost efficiency metrics. It is not a surprise that many of the low cost and ultra-low-cost U.S. airlines are operating at low margins, if they are profitable at all. While American is a global carrier like Delta and United, AAL’s margins have been below low-cost carrier Southwest.

Preying on the weak

United has been very vocal about attempting to win over passengers that low and ultra-low-cost carriers have used to fill their planes. United was the first network carrier to eliminate change fees during covid, a strategy that they positioned as making it easier for passengers to make travel plans during a period of health uncertainty; in reality, the move hasn’t been rescinded and is undoubtedly part of UAL’s success in winning over customers in major cities like Chicago, Denver and Houston where LUV and UAL both have large operations. UAL execs also said they plan to fill extra seats on their larger aircraft as part of their domestic growth strategy with ultra-low-cost carrier passengers, offering them better value.

Spirit Airlines was the first economic casualty with a chapter 11 filing last year and re-emergence this under private equity control. However, Spirit has historically been larger in markets where American and Delta are larger. Delta has previously noted that it will continue to add capacity as necessary to essentially ensure that ultra-low cost carriers cannot grow but it is not clear how the Atlanta-based megacarrier will strategize around deep discount capacity after saying it will reduce capacity. United’s greatest overlap with an ultra-low cost carrier is with Frontier (ULCC) which has recently noted weakness beginning in May, leading to its plans to reduce capacity in the second quarter. ULCC also has little insight into the back half of the year.

The sudden gloom in the airline industry and the unfinished restructuring from covid on top of the restructuring of Spirit raises the question if there will be further consolidation in the U.S. airline industry. United execs expressed on their most recent earnings call that they would be interested in talking with JetBlue (JBLU) if the NYC-based carrier was open to discussions. JBLU has major operations at New York Kennedy, an airport that a previous UAL leadership team left in order to consolidate UAL’s long-haul presence at Newark. UAL’s current leadership team recognizes that leaving JFK airport was a mistake since it is the preferred NYC airport for long-haul travel. JBLU also is a major carrier at Boston and in Florida; while UAL touts its global size, it is the sixth largest airline in the Sunshine State. It is far from clear if further airline industry consolidation will be allowed by the current administration beyond the Alaska-Hawaii merger which just occurred but United appears to be ready to start plugging some of the holes in its network should opportunities arise.

Russian Airspace Reopening?

As the largest U.S. international airline, United has been the most impacted U.S. airline because of closure of Russian airspace to western airlines as a result of Russia’s invasion of Ukraine. President Trump’s desire to end the conflict raises the potential for Russian airspace to be reopened, saving as much as two hours of flying time between parts of Asia and the U.S. In addition, many European and Asian airlines fly well south of the routings they used before the Ukraine war adding time and expense to flights between those regions.

Before the Russia-Ukraine war, United operated up to a half dozen flights per day from the U.S. to India but can only operate just a single flight/day between Newark and Delhi, India operated by a Boeing (BA) 787; the circuitous path that U.S.-India flights have to take pushes the capabilities of United’s fleet. The dashed line in the flight tracking map indicates the most direct path between Delhi and Newark while the green line indicates the actual flight path on a recent day, adding almost 800 miles and pushing the time in the air over 16 hours on some days. Much of the traffic that U.S. airlines cannot carry to India is instead flying via their European partner hubs, via Middle East hubs, or on Air India which does not respect Russia airspace embargoes. Pending the availability of aircraft, United is certain to add more India flights if Russian airspace reopens to U.S. carriers.

Lower fuel prices

United, like all airlines, is a heavy user of petroleum products. In 2024, UAL burned more than 4 billion gallons of jet fuel, costing almost $12 billion or roughly 22% of UAL’s expenses. However, 2024’s fuel bill was down 7% year over year and down 19% in the 4th quarter of 2024 even though it is consuming about 5% more fuel year over year. Delta’s fuel bill for the first quarter of 2025 was down 7%.

Crude oil has continued its decline in 2025, falling 14% since the beginning of the year and 10% in just the last month. Amidst revenue uncertainty, lower fuel costs could help minimize profit declines.

The disadvantages of a large global network

While United’s competitive position and the potential exists to add even more international flights to its impressive international route system, there are significant risks that United must overcome in the present environment. First among the risks facing United is the very likely decrease in international travel particularly to the United States from many of the countries that have historically been strong sources of U.S. tourism. The U.S. and Europe have historically experienced fairly balanced air traffic flows. While data is still fluid, there are growing indications that visitors from some countries are reducing their travel to the U.S. Tariff issues and U.S. remarks denigrating the sovereignty of Canada and Greenland have soured many Canadians and Danes on travel to the U.S. while foreign news has covered stories of immigrant expulsions and increased immigration interrogations at U.S. airports. Some sources indicate that foreign visitor arrivals may already be down by double digit percentages including from Germany, home of Lufthansa German Airlines (OTCQX:DLAKY), UAL’s joint venture and location of United’s largest European operations. The relatively strong dollar continues to support strong tourism by Americans to Europe which occurs generally in the first half of the summer; European tourism to the U.S. increases in the latter half of the summer and in the winter and might be at more risk. As the currently largest transatlantic carrier but with a smaller domestic network than its peers, United could experience a disproportionate decline in revenue if fears of declines in European tourism are sustained.

Travel between Asia and the U.S. is more heavily weighted to Asian originating passengers but the U.S. appears to be more conciliatory in talking about making trade deals with most Asian countries other than China. Flights between the U.S. and China have remained at a fraction of their pre-covid levels but demand could still fall further if relations and trade deteriorate further.

International travel has never been proportionately as profitable as domestic flights for U.S. airlines; United could have ridden a recent three year high in international demand that could be once again waning.

Financial commitments remain high

As I discussed in my previous article, United has retired a fairly low number of aircraft even though its fleet is one of the oldest in the world among major carriers. It has received far fewer aircraft than it had hoped but it also has an aircraft order book that is larger than the rest of the U.S. airline industry combined. It is only because Airbus (OTCPK:EADSY) and Boeing have experienced delivery delays due to their own manufacturing or due to other suppliers including engine makers that United has not spent as much as it had previously planned to spend on capex. If UAL receives all of the aircraft it has on order for the next couple of years, its cash needs far exceed its projected available cash, meaning that taking on additional debt or reducing its cash is likely. Aircraft manufacturing supply chains are expected to see improvement in the next year meaning that United could see an inflow of new aircraft – and an increase in spending – just as the industry weakens. United is also invested tens of millions of dollars on new and expanded airport terminals to support its planned growth, but again far larger than the rest of the industry combined. Even if demand remains strong, United’s terminal expansion will result in higher user costs than for other airlines that are using older facilities.

UAL also has an outstanding stock buyback plan; the majority of the $1.5 billion remains outstanding unless the company is taking advantage of the decreased stock price to buyback stock.

United’s labor costs are certain to increase

Also, as I previously noted, United’s labor costs are likely to grow. By the summer, nearly all of its unionized non-pilot workforce will be working under amendable contracts. Airline labor contracts do not expire so unions negotiate with management until a new contract is reached. American was the most recent airline to settle with major labor groups, adding hundreds of millions of new expenses and paying over $500 million in retroactive payments to its flight attendants. United’s flight attendant workforce is similarly sized to American’s and represents about half of the United employees that could see substantial pay raises and retroactive payments. In addition, airline employees that believe they are underpaid become unhappy employees and customer service deteriorates.

An earnings beat is likely

UAL is expected to release its earnings after market close on Tuesday. Although other airlines have revised their first quarter guidance, UAL said its guidance would be at the low end of its guidance. UAL has a fairly strong history of beating analyst estimates over the last two years.

Wall Street analysts are the most optimistic about UAL while the Seeking Alpha Quant rating remains as a HOLD.

UAL stock has fallen 33% in less than two months since I wrote my last article about the company. Predictable near-term financial performance by Delta, United’s track record for beating analyst estimates, and UAL’s likely ability to weather the likely industry downturn better than its peers, even at the cost of balance sheet stress, merits a return of UAL to a BUY.

data.title: JAAA: A Safe Haven With Bond-Like Yields
data.description:
I recently wrote an article about JAAA, a Collateralised Loan Obligation ("CLO") fund, which I recommended as a smart way to generate a 6% yield. My proposal was to hedge interest rate risk by pairing with Realty Income (O) which has inverse rate risk exposure.

My original article provides a detailed explanation of CLOs, how they operate, and profiled the key risks. I won’t repeat that in this article, but would recommend interested readers click the link, not just for the article, but for a great discussion thread where there is a lot to learn.

JAAA is my preferred CLO investment, because it has a good balance of yield and risk. The current yield is just over 6%, and the risk profile is, as the name implies, AAA quality. This is possible because JAAA invests only in the highest tranches of CLOs which individually are rated AAA by the rating agencies.

The investment case is that JAAA is a great place to store cash, as it offers a spread over cash rates which is higher than the risk of loss of capital justifies.

The one catch, is that, compared to cash, CLO funds like JAAA are exposed to liquidity squeezes, which drop the market price of the fund below the Net Asset Value ("NAV").

The mechanism is, that when there is market stress, investors want, or need to access cash, perhaps to cover margin or other obligations, or simply to ‘raise cash and reduce risk’. Liquidity disappears from the marketplace like water down the plug-hole.

This showed through clearly in recent price action on JAAA around “Liberation Day” and the associated tariff driven market turmoil.

JAAA price has dropped about 1.7% since April 1st. Note however that there was a divided payment date April 1st which accounts for about 0.4% of the drop.

The rest of the drop is mostly due to the shift in market price from a slight premium to NAV, to a material discount. Look at how the discount bounced right back to a 0.6% premium on April 8-9th, and then dropped back to a 0.6% discount as of Friday 11th.

The shift mirrors the general ‘risk on-risk off’ market sentiment as reflected in the SPY pricing.

And you can see clearly the big spike in trading volume in the beginning of April.

The last view I want to show is the correlation between price and volume for JAAA.

From where I am sitting, this doesn’t make sense folks. For the price to NAV of a cash equivalent to bounce up and down like a yo-yo in line with daily market sentiment seems inexplicable.

It’s obvious to me that there is no sophisticated technical driver for this, such as concerns around the underlying CLO NAV valuation. As long as the yield spread to risk free is maintained, an investor can have high confidence that the market price will revert to NAV.

In fact, my expectation is that following such a disruptive event, and with bond yields moving up, its likely that CLO spreads will head up as well.

Conclusion

  • JAAA is a strong place to store cash with minimal risk and an out-sized return.
  • There’s a price tag to accessing that cash when the market is risk off and liquidity is at a premium.
  • This shows up in a temporary NAV discount which seems highly correlated to the market appetite for risk assets.
  • Seeing this work in practice has assuaged my concerns around the price drop in JAAA.
  • JAAA remains a buy as a low risk, high yield bond proxy.
  • A good time to buy is when there is a NAV discount, however the opportunity is tens of basis points rather than tens of percent.

data.title: Chimera Preferreds: Preferred Stocks Are A Buy Again
data.description:
Introduction

Back in June 2022, I started suggesting that investors get ready to invest in preferred stock and baby bonds. It was clear to me that the Federal Reserve was going to push interest rates higher to tamp out inflation and would eventually have to do the reverse to get back to a neutral interest rate after beating down inflation. As the Federal Reserve interest rate cycle progressed, I published no fewer than 12 additional articles on fixed income securities including individual preferred stocks, baby bonds, and preferred stock ETFs. I'm not sure how many SA members followed my advice as the page views for some of those article were rather anemic. I did follow my own advice and made an absolute killing by investing in beaten down preferred stock in the fall of 2022 through spring 2023. I earned double digit total returns on every single security with a few exceeding 50% in total return. I started off with 27 different securities, most of which were fixed-to-floating rate preferred stocks. I still hold 15 of the original 27, the other 12 have been called at various times since September 2022.

Three or four months ago, I was planning for divesting from the 15 preferred stocks I still hold as I thought most would either be called as interest rates continued to fall or I'd end up selling some due to the lower future gain potential. Today, I'm actually adding to a few of those holdings and may continue to do so. This article explains why many preferred stocks are again investible.

Preferred Stocks Are Down

A lot has happened in the last six months. We had a little whiff of higher inflation during the last quarter of 2024 and first quarter of 2025 and it took preferred stock valuations down a notch as investors were concerned that interest rates might not fall as fast as originally envisioned. That valuation impact was fairly minor and did not cause me to rethink my divestiture plans. As a proxy, the chart of PFF below shows the preferred ETF's valuation peaking on October 16, 2024 at $33.51 per share. On March 1, PFF's valuation had fallen to 31.85, a drop of $1.66 or about 5%.

Today, approximately five weeks later, PFF is down to $29, an additional 7.3% down as shown in the following chart.

The significant drop in PFF is reflected in all preferred stock ETFs and the individual preferred stocks I hold. It is probably impossible to know exactly why preferred stocks in general have dropped in valuation over the last roughly 7 months but I have a couple educated guesses.

Fear of inflation returning and the Federal Reserve, rather than lowering interest rates and loosening monetary policy, will have to keep interest rates at the current level or possibly raise them and shift to a tighter monetary policy. With all the media speculation on the potential impacts of higher import tariffs, I can understand the fear. However, I doubt that the US is going to experience a reigniting of inflation due to tariffs. Tariffs are not inherently inflationary. Tariffs may cause a one-time increase in consumer prices but they do not result in continual upward pressure month after month. I said "may cause a one-time price increase" because contrary to popular media opinion, tariff levies are seldom passed through 100% to the end consumer. More typically, tariff levies end up being shared by the manufacturer, importer, retailer, and the consumer. Additionally, tariff levies have economic impacts similar to a consumption tax. Taxes take money from the private sector and transfers it to the government. Tariffs have a similar impact. When was the last time an increase in income taxes caused a bout of inflation? I believe the US is more at risk of a mild consumer driven recession as tariffs result in less consumption and consumer spending. Two thirds of US GDP is driven by consumer spending.

In addition to investors fear of continued or higher inflation, I believe some of the drop in the equity markets, including preferred stocks, is due to panic selling. The velocity and depth of the market moves in the last few days certainly appears to me to have a panic selling component.

Unless you are of the opinion that the end result from the imposition of tariffs will be market armageddon and the end of investing as we know it, the changes over the last 7 months has provided investors with the opportunity to buy high yielding preferred stocks at discounted prices. I firmly believe the US markets are resilient and, in a few years, today's chaos will be but a blip on the charts.

What Am I Buying?

I have kept the same spreadsheet I created to sort through preferred stocks back in the fall of 2022. It has been shortened with fewer entries since that time due to a dozen of the original securities having been called. While it is a shorter list, there are again some good investments to be made in that list. Below is a SNIP/Paste of my EXCEL spreadsheet with prices and other metrics updated with prices as of mid-afternoon Friday, April 11.

The spreadsheet chart above requires a bit of explanation to be useful to everyone. So, from left to right, I'll define/explain the columns that are not obvious.

  • Fixed Payment - current annual payment based on the fixed or floating interest rate as applicable.
  • Yield % - (Fixed Payment)/(Current Price).
  • Coupon Rate - The current fixed rate (not floating yet) or Reset Rate (floating)
  • Year to Call - If negative, the call date is past and the security is callable.
  • Yield to Call or $25 - If call date is negative, entry is capital gain to $25. If call date is positive, YTC.
  • Reset Rate - The current coupon rate if reset at today's CME 3m Term SOFR.

Readers should also note that all but one of the preferreds in the list is LIBOR based. The LIBOR at the top of the chart is synthetic, calculated as SOFR + 0.26%. The single SOFR based preferred, ABR-PF, has the correction to SOFR embedded in the spreadsheet cell.

I typically publish articles after making decisions and initiating or adding to existing holdings. At this time, I've only added to two positions and I'm still considering what other securities I will add to my existing holdings. Earlier this week, I added significantly to my holdings of CIM-PC and CIM-PD. I added to CIM-PD because the current yield is north of 11% and I stand to pick up another 13% when valuations return to near par. I added to CIM-PC because of the high YTC, being callable after September 30, 2025. I'm also considering adding to my holdings of MFA-PC, TWO-PC, and RITM-PC. Readers should note this is only a partial list of what is currently available in the broader market and your risk tolerance may be greater or lesser than mine. Rather than limit yourself to only those on my list, you may wish to generate your own candidate securities starting with the full lists of preferred stocks available on Quantum Online. That is where I started my research originally.

Potential Risks

As I noted above, I don't believe that we are on a path to resurging inflation. With energy prices falling and unemployment rising, albeit slowly, I don't see any catalyst to reignite inflation. I do believe that the current tariffs in place have the potential to lower consumer spending and thus lead to a mild recession. If the economy does turn down sufficiently to recession levels, the bad news is that joblessness will increase causing economic stress for a lot of Americans. Those same conditions, however, will push the Federal Reserve to a more accommodative monetary policy and lower interest rates to stimulate economic activity. Those same lower rates will be push preferred stock valuations up and yields down.

Disclaimer: This article is not investment advice. This article is intended to provide my opinion to interested readers and to serve as a vehicle to generate informed discussion in the comment postings. I have no knowledge of individual investor circumstances, goals, portfolio concentration or diversification. Readers are strongly encouraged to complete their own due diligence on any stock, bond, fund or other investment vehicle mentioned in this article before investing.

data.title: Frontier Airlines Stock Falls On Reduced Consumer Confidence Impact
data.description:
On the 10th of April, Frontier Group (ULCC) issued a guidance update for the first quarter of 2025 incorporating the impact of reduced consumer confidence and pulled its full year guidance as a result.

In this report, I will discuss the guidance adjustment and update my price target for the stock. The last time I calculated a price target for the stock was in July 2024. In that report, I marked the stock a buy but also highlighted the risks in the cost basis of the airline and risks have grown significantly since then.

Lower Consumer Confidence Hits Airlines

For the first quarter, Frontier Airlines is aiming for five percent growth in revenues as well as capacity indicating stable average fares. I believe that these fares may remain somewhat stable relative to capacity deployment driven by ancillary revenues with some pressure on base fares.

Frontier Airlines did not provide a lot of details in its initial guidance issued in February. For Q1 2025, it only indicated a break-even EPS at the low end and a $0.07 EPS at the high end. The preliminary results that the company shared are now indicating a loss of $0.20 to $0.24 per share, which is substantially worse than the initial guidance.

For the second quarter, the company is now expecting to reduce capacity year-on-year through reductions in off-peak flying. For the full year, the company has pulled its guidance that targeted $1.00 in earnings per share.

The fact that Frontier Airlines retracted its full year guidance and would miss its EPS guidance for the first quarter is not surprising. Delta Air Lines (DAL) also had to pull its guidance due to the macroeconomic uncertainty.

Due to the trade war, recession risks have risen, and consumer confidence has fallen. Airlines are very susceptible to swings in consumer confidence, so the 17 points decline from the end of 2024 until March is not a good sign and coupled with the close-in booking nature of Frontier Airlines that provides a significant near-term erosion of revenues and results.

It is yet another sign that policies proposed and implemented by the current administration are putting a significant pressure on consumer behavior.

Frontier Airlines Already Had A Hard Time Before The Trade Saga

For ultra-low-cost carriers, it already hasn’t been easy coming out of the pandemic. The pilot shortage led to mainline carriers increasing pilot pay, and, in many occasions, also higher pay for technicians and flight attendants.

Ultra-low-cost carriers are built on the fundamentals of low-cost, with capacity scaling to run more efficiently. That low-cost nature has been eroded. There always is the volatility of fuel prices, but the staff pay, inflation on navigation and landing fees and raw material costs resulting in higher maintenance costs effectively increased the cost basis for airlines.

Due to a combination of pilot and aircraft shortages, low-cost carriers have also been less able to scale the business to drive down unit costs and increase pricing pressure. As a result, the companies have lost competitive strength against mainline carriers.

Whereas mainline carriers often can rely on product segmentation and international long-haul operations, low-cost carriers often do not have those levers available, leaving them more exposed to increases in cost dynamics.

If you add that the US domestic market already showed signs of saturation last year and at the reduced consumer confidence now, airlines are left with a high cost basis and lower demand and/or lower unit revenues.

ULCC Stock - Challenged Business Concept Commands Hold Rating

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Frontier Airlines, there does seem to be upside. However, given the challenges from a cost and market demand perspective, I believe a hold rating is more appropriate. For 2024, the company achieved EBITDA in line with expectations but substantially better on free cash flow due to lower CapEx. For 2025, the estimates have decreased by 14% for EBITDA and the cash burn is expected to be higher due to deliveries slipping from 2025 into 2026. For 2026, the EBITDA has been trimmed by 25%.

Overall, there seems to be a shift of one year in the expected earnings. With that in mind, I am assigning a $6.50 price target with a hold rating. Despite the significant upside, the risk profile does not make it attractive enough to mark the stock a buy.

Conclusion: Frontier Airlines Unappealing Upside Due To Risks

Frontier Airlines is facing a lot of uncertainty this year, and we also do not know whether the current trade environment will improve and positively affect the macroeconomic picture. That is why I have a hold rating on the name despite the significant upside. It would be a speculative buy at best. There has been a lot of pressure on costs for low-cost carriers without positive differentiators to offset the costs efficiently. We’re now also seeing weaker market demand with an uncertain outlook, which does not make things better for Frontier Airlines.

Airlines are very susceptible to swings in consumer confidence. Frontier Airlines definitely is not an exception to that rule or the only victim of erosion in consumer confidence. However, the cost development witnessed over the past three years or so makes the operational environment exponentially more challenging for low-cost and ultra-low-cost carriers.

data.title: Intercontinental Exchange: Dividend Growth Opportunity With Possible Headwinds
data.description:
Intercontinental Exchange: Dividend Growth Opportunities with Possible Headwinds

Intercontinental Exchange, Inc. (NYSE:ICE) is a market leader in exchanges, fixed-income services and data, and mortgage technology. It owns the valuable NYSE brand. Moreover, the company is an interesting dividend growth stock. The dividend yield is low, the dividend safety is excellent, and Intercontinental Exchange has a 12-year growth streak, making it a Dividend Contender. Despite the attractive characteristics and growth prospects, the valuation is slightly pricey, and leverage is higher than desired. Hence, I view Intercontinental Exchange as a hold.

Overview of Intercontinental Exchange

Intercontinental Exchange, Inc. was founded in 2000 as an operator of financial exchanges. Today, it has three business segments: Exchanges, Fixed Income and Data Services, and Mortgage Technology. The firm owns the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) and operates derivatives, futures, and commodity exchanges. Intercontinental Exchange also offers pricing and market data, risk management services, and trading support. The company expanded into mortgage technology services after acquiring Ellie Mae in 2020 and Black Knight in 2023.

Total revenue was over $9,279 million in 2024 and in the last twelve months (“LTM”).

Revenue and Earnings Growth

The firm announced mixed third-quarter fiscal year 2025 results on February 6th, 2025, beating earnings consensus but missing revenue estimates. Revenue climbed 5% to $2,323 million from $2,201, beating forecasts by a wide amount of ~$952 million. Revenue rose with increased trading, some fixed-income services, and mortgage closings and services. However, some business lines experienced declines. Diluted adjusted earnings per share (“EPS”) of $1.52 were ahead of estimates by $0.03 on higher revenue, even though margins were pressured in the mortgage segment. A slightly higher diluted share count was a headwind.

The share price was flat, but it has been increasing since the beginning of the year before the recent decline instigated by tariffs. The firm is forecasting low-to-mid single-digit revenue growth in 2025. The share price has gained ~4.6% year-to-date (“YTD”) and ~16.8% in the past twelve months.

Intercontinental Exchange’s revenue has increased annually from fiscal year 2015 to 2024. Intercontinental Exchange is growing organically because of its expanded offerings in market and pricing data and increased trading.

The company also conducts aggressive M&A and is thus a significant growth driver. It has bought several firms, including NYSE Euronext in 2013, Interactive Data in 2015, Ellie Mae in 2020, and Black Knight in 2023, adding to the top line. We do not expect significant acquisitions in the near term because of debt and leverage. Consequently, revenue has risen to $9.28 billion in FY 2024 from $3.34 billion in FY 2015.

The company should continue its growth in fiscal 2025 and beyond. Revenue is expected to rise about 6% per year in 2025 and 2026. Even though we do not expect large acquisitions, enhanced volatility because of tariffs should drive trading and fixed-income revenue. However, higher interest rates may negatively affect mortgage services revenue. That said, the firm makes consistent technology investments and improvements, increasing its offerings and subscriber base. Also, Intercontinental Exchange is combining its mortgage capabilities into a single ecosystem, which should improve the value proposition and, thus, revenue.

Adjusted EPS has followed a similar trend to revenue. It has increased from $2.43 in 2015 to $6.07 in 2024. EPS is expected to reach $6.81 in FY 2025, more than in 2024, and grow to $7.59 in 2026. Intercontinental Exchange’s share repurchase program is suspended because of leverage. The firm is reducing debt and leverage as a priority. Because the company uses shares for M&A, the count has increased since 2020. However, we expect buybacks to resume after leverage is reduced.

Looking forward, I expect this market leader to continue growing over time, primarily on trading volatility, organic subscriber growth, and mortgage servicing. The firm’s customers require Intercontinental Exchange’ value-added products and services to generate returns and reduce risk.

Recent Challenges and Risks

Intercontinental Exchange’s primary risk is an economic downturn that could negatively impact its customers. During the subprime mortgage crisis, the firm's bottom line fell steeply before bouncing back. Also, higher interest rates will affect house sales and, thus, closings and servicing volumes.

In terms of competition, Intercontinental Exchange is a market leader. That said, its main competitors are equally as large without the distraction of a mortgage business. Moreover, they are focused on exchanges and trading. Also, the barrier to entry is not high, and the SEC has approved a new exchange in Texas backed by major financial firms. Also, crypto exchanges could feasibly expand into other areas.

For now, we expect tariffs to have an impact on Intercontinental Exchange because they seem to be affecting interest rates. If this trend is sustained, it will cause mortgage rates to become elevated and thus affect home sales.

Competitive Advantages

Intercontinental Exchange's competitive advantage is its scale in trading, the NYSE brand, proprietary products based on data from its exchanges, commodity, futures, and fixed-income data, and a complete suite of mortgage servicing products. This probably gives the company a narrow moat.

Dividend Analysis

Intercontinental Exchange’s increasing share price has caused the dividend yield to decline from its peak in late 2022. It is near its 5-year average of 1.28%. The forward yield is about 1.24%, even after the recent market decline. This implies a fairly valued equity.

Intercontinental Exchange is a Dividend Contender with a 12-year streak of increases. In the past 5-years, the growth rate has been approximately 10.4%. It was greater in the past decade at about 13.2% because of increasing payouts. The conservative payout ratio of ~30% indicates more future dividend increases. However, we expect a lower growth rate because of debt requirements. The firm gave investors a 6.7% increase in 2024.

The dividend is supported by excellent safety. The forward payout ratio is very conservative at 30%, based on an estimated consensus 2025 EPS of $6.81. This value is definitely below my requirement of 65% or better. The ratio may decrease over the next few years because of incremental dividend increases compared to EPS growth. The firm’s free cash flow (“FCF”) has trended upward since 2020 because of top-line growth. It was $2,674 million in 2020 and rose to $4,203 million in 2024. That said, the FCF covers the dividend requirement of about $1,039 million. Assuming $4,500 million of FCF and $1,100 million in dividends this year gives a dividend-to-FCF ratio of ~24.4%, well below our desired value of 70% or better.

Intercontinental Exchange’s balance sheet is leveraged. It carries $529 million of short-term and $2,498 million of current long-term debt. Long-term debt is $17,341 million. It holds about $844 million in cash, equivalents, and investments. As a result, the leverage ratio is 3.46X, and interest coverage is only 4.86X.

Intercontinental Exchange also receives a ‘B+’ dividend quality grade from Portfolio Insight, placing it in the 80th percentile. It measures earnings performance, revenue performance, dividend performance, profitability, and financial strength.

Consequently, investors should be highly confident about the safety of Intercontinental Exchange’s dividends. I expect payments to continue increasing in the mid-single digits until leverage is reduced.

Valuation

Intercontinental Exchange's current share price and anticipated EPS in 2025 have caused the valuation to decrease. It is currently at ~22.8X, within its 5-year and 10-year ranges.

Consensus estimates for adjusted diluted earnings are $6.81 per share in 2025, $0.74 more than in 2024. We will use 21X as a reasonable, fair-value multiple in the middle of its usual range, accounting for current economic uncertainty and ad hoc policies. As a result, our fair value estimate is $143.01. The present share price is ~$155.91, indicating that Intercontinental Exchange is overvalued.

Applying a sensitivity calculation using P/E ratios between 20X and 22X, we obtain a fair value range from $136.20 to $149.82. Hence, the stock price is approximately 104% to 114% of the fair value estimate.

Estimated Current Valuation Based On P/E Ratio

P/E Ratio

20

21

22

Estimated Value

$136.20

$143.01

$149.82

% of Estimated Value at Current Stock Price

114%

109%

104%

Source: dividendpower.org Calculations

How does this calculation compare to other valuation models? Portfolio Insight's blended fair value model, combining the P/E ratio and dividend yield, estimates a fair value of $159.05 per share. The two-model average is ~$151.03, indicating that Intercontinental Exchange is slightly overvalued at the current price.

However, Wall Street analysts are bullish, with a price target of $188.31, 20.78% higher than the current value and more than our two-model average. Analysts are generally optimistic in their ratings, with seven strong buys, seven buys, four holds, zero sells, and zero strong sells. However, the Seeking Alpha Quant system rating is a hold because of unfavorable valuation and growth. However, profitability, momentum, and revisions are favorable.

Final Thoughts

Intercontinental Exchange is a market leader that grows organically and by aggressive M&A. It offers products and services in three different areas, providing some diversification. However, the financial focus means that the firm will be challenged during times of economic duress. Also, I view tariffs as a risk to the mortgage business, but they are positive from the perspective of market volatility. That said, the firm's execution has resulted in consistently rising revenue and earnings in the past decade. The firm's dividend safety and streak, business model, and growth are attractive. A downside is debt and leverage. However, this stock would fit in satisfactorily with many dividend growth portfolios. That said, the current valuation is slightly elevated. Hence, I view Intercontinental Exchange as a hold.

data.title: Nvidia Stock Is Trading As If It's 2019 Again
data.description:
Introduction

Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) managed to go up by a factor of >20 from April 2020 to mid-January 2025 before the recent correction and given its stably high valuation multiples amid the market's rising concerns about DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) and the US-China trade war, it was a question of time when we see a cash-out of "the peak-catchers". From December 31st to April 4th, the stock fell by ~30% (now following the recent relief rally, NVDA is down -17% on a YTD basis), and that correction was triggered by the general weakness of the market (and the tech niche in particular).

My Thesis Update

I first wrote about NVDA back in late February 2024 with a "Hold" rating, mainly discussing the investment cycles and their impacts on cyclical semiconductor stocks. After some upgrades and downgrades, following the actual data from the firm and the industry, my most recent rating remained bullish. In late January 2025, I argued that the DeepSeek launch was actually a tailwind for Nvidia, not an operational risk, and since that update, the NVDA stock is down just 6.32%, beating the S&P 500 (SPX) (SP500) index on a relative basis:

Given that the firm managed to release its fiscal Q4 FY2025 in late February and is still undergoing a correction, I think I should look at my rating again and assess whether NVDA deserves a downgrade this time.

So let's soberly take a look at the fundamentals, not follow the fear's narratives. I don't see a clear hit to the company's market position in the next few years, as the demand will likely keep expanding tremendously even from today's relatively higher base. What's also important - the stock has finally become a real bargain at its current price when we consider the forwarding estimates and apply a premium to them based on the historical trends. This is why I'm reiterating my "Buy" today.

My Updated Reasoning

No matter what fears try to tell us right now, the facts are here - the demand environment stays strong with Nvidia reporting full-year revenue of $130.5 billion (10-K released in late February), which is a 114% YoY increase. The Q4 top-line hit ~$35.6 billion (up 93% YoY), which is showing some deceleration compared to the previous quarters' growth rates, but the firm's transition to the Blackwell architecture and the expanding scope of AI applications seem to be just getting started. I mean, delivering ~$11 billion in Blackwell revenue in its first quarter, exceeding the management's internal expectations quite meaningfully, and it suggests an intense customer demand and Nvidia's supply chain execution, even amidst some obstacles. At the same time, NVDA's customers "are finishing builds or need immediate compute while awaiting Blackwell", as the CFO noted during the recent Morgan Stanley conference, so the existing Hopper H200 platform continues to sell well. From that, I see limited sales cannibalization from Blackwell - if that were the case, the Hopper H200 platforms wouldn't continue to see sequential growth in Q4 FY25 (everyone would be just waiting for the newest offering). As some data center build-outs were designed around the Hopper architecture, it's going to feel continued demand - that's why I think once Blackwell production fully ramps up and its supply becomes more widely available, we're unlikely to see a sharper deterioration in Nvidia's consolidated top-line growth rate from here on.

Given all that, the Wall Street analysts' forecasts regarding Nvidia's revenues seem a bit pessimistic when we consider post-2026 estimates: From FY2026 to FY2030, analysts see an expansion in sales by just 52%, while the Generative AI market's size is projected to increase by a factor of ~2.6 over the same time (based on Grand View Research's study).

That's why I'd bet on at least a few years of positive revenue surprises, akin to the 2020-2025 period:

Now turning my sight to the bottom-line expectations, I see that from FY2026 to FY2030, the market expects a cumulative expansion in Nvidia's EPS of ~55%, so there's going to be a minimal operating leverage and margin expansion (the market probably assumes that the current margins are already at peak).

Indeed, visually Nvidia's margins do look like they're peaking:

The fears of peaking margins are real because Blackwell GPUs are way more complex than Hopper (208 billion vs. 80 billion transistors), use a custom, likely more expensive TSMC 4NP process initially, and incorporate advanced packaging, especially the GB200 integrating a CPU and two GPUs. So cost-wise, they're going to carry higher COGS for the firm, and hence that might bring challenges in transferring those costs to the end buyers. We already saw Blackwell's impact on the margins in Q4 when Nvidia's GP margins dipped into the low 70s% range during "the initial, expedited Blackwell ramp". The company will have a wider pricing range depending on specs, but the basic B100 is priced similarly to the H100 ($30-35k vs $30-40k) despite offering substantially better performance - that's why I wouldn't cancel off the market's concern entirely. Anyway, I think the gradual shift in the sales mix towards higher-priced GB200 Superchips (at $60-70k) will be a game changer for Nvidia. If Blackwell delivers, say, 3-5x the performance or efficiency for ~2x the price, the value proposition will remain compelling and support the margins at least where they are now.

Also, the Q4 dip in margin was due to the initial ramp, so assumingly, once production scales, manufacturing yields improve, the TSMC 4NP process matures, and Nvidia gains economies of scale, the per-unit COGS for Blackwell should decrease, and so one of the marginality pressures would vanish in the medium term.

So given all that, if we assume that today's marginality is indeed at a peak, but this peak doesn't necessarily mean that the margin contraction is coming, then in this case the EBITDA generation (and hence the flow-through to FCF) will likely stay in place.

With the same level of EBITDA transferring to the FCF, Nvidia's cash capacity for increasing buybacks will be there. In FY2025, the firm spent ~$40.6 billion on share repurchases, which, according to Seeking Alpha Premium, is about 2.3 times more than during FY2024. With margins staying where they are now (or even a bit lower), the firm's FCF would be enough to buy back more and hence reduce the denominator for the forwarding EPS more meaningfully compared to what seems to be priced in right now.

It's impossible to ignore the geopolitical concerns, and the US-China trade war in particular right now. The US export controls (before the implementation of all the tariffs) have already lowered sales to China - the CFO Colette Kress noted that "revenue percentage from the region is roughly half of what it was before the controls". Also, the recently announced "tariff exemptions" on smartphones, electronics, and chips - what helped the NVDA and the whole market close the previous week in green - "will be short-lived", The Guardian cited Trump in its just-published note:

So with PCBs, memory modules, and different chip components assembled in China (and also South Asia), the firm's COGS might stay under pressure, for sure. But at the same time, as we're talking about the geopolitics here, I should note that "sovereign AI initiatives" are taking hold as well, with countries like India and Japan making massive investments in Nvidia-powered AI factories to build national capabilities. As the world's globalization is gradually replaced by deglobalization with major countries trying to secure their national independence (in tech, AI, supply chain, etc.), I believe Nvidia's offerings will stay in high demand, and that demand won't be as elastic as most consumer goods (because the consumers in this case are governments with much more resources).

So far, the fundamentals of Nvidia's business seem to be exceptional, and even if we look at NVDA's stock price chart, we see a clear high-probability setup for a great reversal to the upside:

At least this is how I interpret the chart.

When it comes to the stock's valuation, I think now it looks way more tempting than at the end of calendar 2024. On a forwarding basis, the stock is valued at just 24.5x earnings, which is the lowest point in the past 6 years. In other words, following the YTD correction, Nvidia stock is currently trading as if it's 2019 again, but the prospects, in my view, look much more visible right now:

Assuming 30x in FWD earnings by the end of FY2026, and implying a premium of just 5% (coming from both the earnings surprises and upward revisions), my price target for NVDA arrives at ~$180.2/share. That's a growth potential of over 62% to the current stock price.

Risks And Your Takeaway

While I'm quite bullish on NVDA, I admit that there are a lot of risks to my thesis update. In case the revenue shift that I've discussed above doesn't lead to higher-priced Blackwell sales and the firm just keeps selling same-priced offerings that come at a higher COGS for them, the margins will continue to fall, and that will definitely hurt the stock's valuation. Also, if the price elasticity for Nvidia's demand isn't at the level I assumed, and the transferring of rising COGS doesn't happen the way I see it, it will translate to lower earnings in the medium term. Lower-than-expected earnings growth for Nvidia will mean a sharp decrease in its stock price.

Despite these obvious risks to my thesis, I believe we should be first of all assess the facts, and the odds of what's likely to happen to the operational business in the next few years. So far, I mostly see positives. Yes, the AI hype is gradually evaporating, but the demand side is strong - without Nvidia's offerings there's no chance to compete, and the big techs seem to understand it well.

Based on my valuation calculations, the stock is undervalued by over 62% (from my PT for the next 12 months), so in case you were looking for an opportunity to buy NVDA cheap, it might be your chance.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: Very Bad News For Realty Income
data.description:
Earlier this month, I discussed Realty Income's (NYSE:O) latest results and noted that the company was facing growing headwinds.

Unfortunately, I have more bad news to share regarding the "monthly dividend company".

You may recall that last year, I shared an article explaining why I thought that President Trump's victory was bad news for the stock.

The reason is tariffs.

They have the potential to hurt Realty Income in two ways.

Firstly, it is expected to increase inflation, which is an issue for Realty Income because most of its leases only include fixed annual rent escalations of 1% or 1.5%. This means that the REIT's revenue will rapidly lose in purchasing power if inflation accelerates as a result of tariffs. This year, the REIT has only guided for 1.4% FFO per share growth, which will likely be quite a lot lower than the rate of inflation.

The second reason why tariffs are bad news for Realty Income is that it is heavily exposed to retail sectors that are expected to face significant pain as a result of tariffs. It already has growing tenant issues, and those have just gotten a lot bigger as a result of President Trump's latest tariff announcements.

In today's Realty Income stock review, I am going to discuss what these latest tariffs mean for the stock, and I will end by sharing my rating for the stock (Buy, Hold, or Sell) and highlighting a few of its peers that might be more attractive.

Tariffs Will Hurt Realty Income's Biggest Tenants

Earlier this month, President Trump imposed very high sweeping tariffs on most other countries in the world.

Then, more recently, he adjusted his plan by lowering the tariffs to 10% on most countries for the next 90 days while increasing the tariffs on China to a whopping 125%!

Bloomberg noted that the overall impact on the US economy should be fairly similar given that the average US tariff rate only dropped a few percentage points:

I fear that this will make Realty Income's growing tenant issues a lot worse.

As a reminder, Realty Income is heavily exposed to Walgreens (WBA), Dollar General (DG), and Dollar Tree (DLTR) today. They are its second, third, and fourth largest tenants, representing them alone about 10% of its rental revenue.

But unfortunately for Realty Income, pharmacies and dollar stores could be some of the worst-impacted retail sectors by Trump's tariff proposals:

Pharmacies have been suffering for years due to oversupply and growing competition from big players like Amazon. As a result, Walgreens (WBA), which is Realty Income's second-biggest tenant, has been closing stores left and right in an attempt to save the business.

The problem is that if your business was hurting before the tariff, then your troubles will only get a lot bigger going forward. Pharmacies import a lot of low-cost goods from countries like China, and a big segment of their clients are low-income households.

In an exclusive interview last year, the CEO of Agree Realty (ADC) predicted to us that Walgreens could eventually have to file for bankruptcy to get rid of many of its properties that are losing money. That's the only way to get out of its long-term leases.

That was before the tariffs.

Now, he is likely growing even more confident in his prediction, and before you discredit him as a fearmonger, you should note that Walgreens used to be Agree Realty's biggest tenant, so few people know this company better than they do,

If Walgreens was going to face severe losses due to the tariffs and eventually file for bankruptcy, it would undoubtedly have a significant negative impact on the market sentiment of Realty Income, given that it is one of its top tenants.

It would slow down its growth as redeveloping and releasing these properties would be costly.

Why not just sell the properties today?

Well, if it was that easy, they would probably do it, but valuations have already dropped significantly. Recently, I have come across more and more listings to buy Walgreens net lease properties at 8%+ cap rates, which is a new high.

Here is another one:

Higher cap rates result in lower property values.

Increasingly, many landlords are becoming concerned and looking to sell these properties, and this is resulting in higher cap rates. The company recently announced that it had come to a deal with private equity firm Sycamore Partners to privatize the company, which will only increase uncertainty since we won't have access to the company's financials for much longer. Also, what is the plan of this private equity firm? Are they just smelling blood and looking to break up the pieces to unlock value?

Only time will tell...

If Realty Income were to sell these properties, it would likely be dilutive since it would then need to reinvest the capital into other properties at lower cap rates.

And, unfortunately for Realty Income, it is not just pharmacies.

The REIT is also heavily exposed to dollar stores, and we have previously argued that those are the "ground zero" for tariffs. That's because they focus on lower-income households and import a lot of cheap products from China and other countries.

Their margins are already very tight, and these tariffs will make things a lot worse. Dollar Tree is particularly heavily impacted because it focuses more on discretionary goods, and it was already struggling and closing stores before the tariffs came into place. Their CEO also recently announced that he was stepping down, and one of their competitors, 99 Cents, filed for bankruptcy.

Will Dollar Tree face the same outcome?

I don't know.

But before tariffs, they were planning to close 1,000 stores in the near term, and things will not get better with tariffs.

Again, if we see more headlines of dollar stores closing down, or worse, one of these big companies filing for bankruptcy, it would likely have a very negative impact on the market sentiment of Realty Income.

Buy, Hold, or Sell?

Realty Income is facing growing tenant issues, and those will only get worse because of tariffs.

It will likely slow down its growth even further as it seeks to proactively sell weaker assets to reduce its exposure.

I fear that its growth will not keep up with inflation, leading to negative real-growth in the dividend payments.

However, the stock is already historically cheap, trading at just 12x FFO and offering a 6% dividend yield, which is compelling coming from an A-rated REIT.

Moreover, if and when we go into a recession, this could push interest rates to lower levels, benefiting net lease REITs like Realty Income.

Therefore, I still expect Realty Income to do relatively well over the long run and would give it a Hold rating.

However, I wouldn't give it a Buy rating because there are better options in its peer group.

Some of its peers enjoy better leases that offer stronger protection against inflation. As an example, W.P. Carey (WPC) and VICI Properties (VICI) both enjoy CPI-based rent escalations, which should result in faster growth in the coming years if tariffs remain in place. Despite that, they trade at fairly similar valuations as Realty Income today.

Moreover, some other peers are also less exposed to struggling retail categories like pharmacies and dollar stores. Good examples here would be Agree Realty (ADC), which focuses on stronger categories and derives most of its revenue from investment-grade-rated tenants.

So to recap: tariffs are bad news for Realty Income, and some of its peers offer better risk-to-reward given that they trade at fairly similar valuations but enjoy better leases and/or are less exposed to the weakest retail categories.

data.title: Apogee Therapeutics: Engineering Biologic Dominance
data.description:
With recent financial disclosures and pipeline updates including encouraging interim Phase 1 data for its OX40L inhibitor APG990, I believe Apogee (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:APGE) is laying the groundwork for significant value creation as a disruptor in multi-billion dollar indications, including atopic dermatitis, asthma, and beyond. This strategy focuses on optimized biologics that provide meaningful differentiation in crowded therapeutic landscapes.

Financials

Apogee's year end financials show a company laying down roots for the long haul. By December 31, 2024, cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities were at $731.1 million. This is a big jump from $395.5 million reported at the end of 2023. Most importantly, management believes this war chest will be enough to fund operating expenses through the first quarter of 2028. Having this extended cash runway is a major de-risking factor for a clinical-stage biotech - it provides time to move multiple pipeline candidates through key clinical milestones without the immediate threat of dilutive financing rounds. Having this financial strength enables the company to develop from a position of stability.

Moving an ambitious pipeline is expensive. Research and development expenses climbed to $167.9 million for the full year of 2024 from $68.4 million in 2023. This increase is not unexpected and necessary. It accurately represents the work done to date, namely the development of the lead IL-13 inhibitor APG777, combination programs APG279 (APG777 + APG990) and the APG777 + APG333 program, and the pipeline moving forward into clinical trials. The other contributors are increased personnel costs - including equity-based compensation tied to a growing research and development team - a sign of scaling operations to meet the pipeline potential.

Similarly, General and administrative expenses nearly doubled to $49.0 million in 2024 from $24.6 million in 2023. This rise reflects higher personnel costs, including equity compensation, as headcount growth was required to support expanding operations and manage the inherent challenges of a public company. Even though higher expenses drive a larger net loss, that pattern is typical of companies at the development stage.

Thus the net loss in 2024 was $182.1 million versus $84.0 million in 2023. This increased loss is caused by increased research and development, as well as general and administrative spending, which fuels pipeline advancement. This was partly offset by higher interest income - probably due to the larger cash position. While profitability is the ultimate goal, I think the current spending reflects a concentrated effort to create value through clinical development with a healthy cash position to cover short term financial risk. A runway that extends into 2028 provides some cushion for data readouts.

Pipeline Expansion

Apogee's pipeline strategy highlights its ambition to target major inflammatory diseases including atopic dermatitis, asthma and eosinophilic esophagitis in differentiated ways. Its core is APG777, an IL-13 inhibitor conceived as potentially the best in monotherapy. It is a well-validated target for Type 2 inflammation, but Apogee seeks to enhance these therapies - perhaps through optimized dosing or efficacy. This atopic dermatitis program has progressed significantly: Part A of the Phase 2 trial has been enrolled, and initial 16-week induction data are expected by mid-2025. That's a key catalyst. More long-term data from Part A (expected in 2026) and subsequent Part B data (H2 in 2026) will further define APG777's profile in this large market. Apogee is now targeting APG777 for use in asthma and eosinophilic esophagitis, with Phase 1b and Phase 2b trials starting in 2025 and data collection in 2026. In this way, the core asset is systematically expanded across relevant indications.

Apogee isn't necessarily reliant on monotherapy success, though. The company is actively exploring combination strategies aiming for first-in-class or best-in-class outcomes while recognizing the heterogeneity of diseases like atopic dermatitis. APG279 - APG777 + APG990, an OX40L inhibitor, is the most advanced combination. OX40L sits further upstream in the inflammatory cascade than IL-13, and may provide broader immunosuppression along the Type 1, Type 2, and Type 3 pathways. The rationale is compelling: Combine the deep Type 2 inhibition of APG777 with the broader activity of APG990 to possibly confer superior efficacy in atopic dermatitis patients who are not responding to single-pathway inhibition. Phase 1b proof-of-concept trial of APG279 is scheduled for 2025 and will include a head-to-head comparison with the current standard-of-care DUPIXENT. Data from this important trial is expected in the second half of 2026 and will be a turning point for the company and its combination strategy.

Apogee is developing another combination combining APG777 with APG333, a TSLP inhibitor. TSLP is another key cytokine associated with inflammatory diseases, including asthma. That combined approach - now in preclinical development for asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease - extends Apogee's strategy to include targeting different disease mechanisms and possibly different patient populations. Its clinical plans are due for release in 2025. Such a multi-pronged approach, including a potentially best-in-class monotherapy and synergistic combination therapies targeting distinct pathways, positions Apogee to carve out a niche in the vast inflammatory and immunology landscape.

APG990 Initial Data Shines

More recent news has reinforced the rationale for Apogee's combination strategy, specifically for APG990. The company reported good interim data from the first-in-human Phase 1 trial of APG990, a subcutaneous, half-life-extended monoclonal antibody targeting OX40L. This trial with 40 healthy adult participants provided important pharmacokinetic and safety data.

The pharmacokinetic profile of APG990 emerges as the main finding from the interim analysis. Data revealed that the half-life of approximately 60 days applies to all tested doses. This is an encouraging result, and supports very infrequent maintenance dosing - perhaps every three or six months - with doses as low as 50 mg. The longer half-life is an integral part of Apogee's engineering strategy and a potential differentiation factor against more frequent-acting therapies. For the APG279 combination, this pharmacokinetic profile supports the use of the coformulated product in single 2 mL injections twice to four times per year. Having a convenient dosing schedule may be a competitive advantage in terms of patient adherence and preference.

Equally important was the tolerability profile from the Phase 1 study. APG990 was tolerated well across five cohorts at doses up to 1,200mg. Like in any trial, treatment-emergent adverse events were observed mainly due to headaches (>=10% incidence). In total, 53% of participants reported at least one treatment-emergent adverse event. Critically, however, no Grade 3 treatment-emergent adverse events related to the study drug were recorded, no severe adverse events, and no adverse events led to study discontinuation. Neither pyrexia nor chills, which are sometimes associated with immune-modulating therapies, were observed. A clean initial safety profile is necessary even for drugs indicated for chronic conditions and as part of a combination therapy.

Apogee leaders understandably stressed the importance of those findings. CEO Michael Henderson noted the extended pharmacokinetic profile and good tolerability that support APG990 as a combination partner with APG777 for atopic dermatitis and other inflammatory diseases. He noted that APG279 may broadly inhibit Type 1, 2, and 3 inflammation, providing possibly greater efficacy with fewer side effects than other therapies. Supporting preclinical toxicology studies with no safety finding, even at 150 mg/kg per agent over 3 months, and potential for enhanced pharmacologic responses supports the case for APG279. Those encouraging results led Henderson to confirm the plan to begin the head-to-head Phase 1b trial of APG279 against DUPIXENT later this year.

The potential for quarterly or less frequent dosing was mentioned, and the therapeutic hypothesis was reiterated. Combining APG990's broad inhibition with APG777's deep Type 2 inhibition may result in an effective but safe treatment approach. These good interim results support the APG990 in the pipeline and de-risk the APG279 combination trial. Now the company plans to file an Investigational New Drug application or its foreign equivalent for APG279, with a Phase 1b trial starting in 2025 and data due in the second half of 2026.

Risks and Competition

The real risk lies in the clinical development itself. Good early-stage data, such as APG990, does not guarantee success in larger, longer Phase 2 or Phase 3 trials. Strange safety signals could emerge, or the drugs may fail to demonstrate statistical significance compared with placebo or active comparators such as DUPIXENT. Regulatory hurdles also pose a risk. FDA and other global health authorities require robust data packages meeting high standards to obtain approval. Common industry delays in trial enrollment, data analysis, or regulatory review can impact timelines and investor sentiment.

Competitive risk is high in inflammatory and immunology. Markets like atopic dermatitis and asthma are stuffed with established players as well as many therapies in development. DUPIXENT represents a major commercial success, and the benchmark Apogee seeks to challenge with APG777 monotherapy and APG279 combination. Other companies develop novel IL-13 inhibitors, OX40/OX40L inhibitors, TSLP inhibitors and other mechanisms. Apogee's differentiation strategy, including extended half-life and infrequent dosing, is critical for competing effectively, but success is dependent on showing clear advantages in clinical trials.

Execution risk is another factor. Managing multiple clinical programs across different indications and development stages requires significant operational expertise. Apogee needs to successfully execute its clinical trials, manage its supply chain and navigate the regulatory landscape to meet its stated timelines. The cash runway is strong now but significant additional capital will be needed for development, possible Phase 3 trials and eventual commercialization. Downstream challenges are also related to market access and reimbursement negotiations. Investors must weigh those risks against the considerable potential market opportunity if Apogee's differentiated candidates prove successful.

Market Valuation And Upside Outlook

The opportunity for atopic dermatitis alone is huge - there are about 26 million U.S. patients and over 100 million worldwide. About 30% have moderate-to-severe disease, which translates to about 7.8 million U.S. patients eligible for biologics. Given that the treatment costs for agents like DUPIXENT range from $35,000-$40,000 per year, a 10% U.S. share for APG777 or APG279 could translate to $2.7-$3.1 billion in revenue. Internationally, assuming 20 million eligible patients and 5% penetration at $30,000 per year, adds another $3 billion, making atopic dermatitis a $6 billion total addressable market under conservative assumptions.

Asthma has additional upside: There are 25 million U.S. patients - 5-10% with severe, uncontrolled disease - 1.25 to 2.5 million. The APG777 + APG333 combo could bring in around $1.8 billion domestically at similar treatment costs, with global expansion reaching $3.5-$4 billion.

Eosinophilic esophagitis, though smaller, is still meaningful. This market is worth more than $6 billion worldwide, with around 180,000 U.S. patients and biologic pricing near $35,000. A small penetration here could generate hundreds of millions annually.

Together, these indications represent a market potential of more than $13 billion annually, excluding future expansion into related diseases or regions. Differentiated biologics with infrequent dosing and broad efficacy could command premium share and pricing for Apogee. Long-duration dosing also provides convenience and a competitive edge.

At around $1.9 billion, Apogee's valuation only captures a small slice of the long-term upside potential. A longer cash runway to 2028, plus a number of near-term catalysts including a critical Phase 2 readout for APG777 support a strong upside case. Apogee isn’t just advancing therapies, but is positioning to lead in the multi-billion dollar inflammatory market.

data.title: Amazon's Retail May Be Vulnerable - Its Cloud Business Is Not
data.description:
Investment Thesis

As a result of Trump's tariffs on China, Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) is canceling import orders from Southeast Asia and China.

In a move seen as cushioning the impact from U.S. trade policy, Amazon (AMZN) has cancelled orders that originate from China and Southeast Asian countries, leaving certain vendors on the hook with unsold inventory.

At first, this sounds pretty bad. It looks like Amazon is cutting back or that something is fundamentally wrong with the commercial segment of its business. But I see it in a slightly different way. Amazon isn't pulling back because of weak demand or operational issues. The company is adapting fast to the changing environment, and this is an advantage. Amazon is flexible, it is adapting, and it's protecting itself from the unpredictable geopolitical environment. This is the behavior I want to see from the company I invest in.

Without a doubt, the trade war with China and the constant changes in policies create short-term headwinds for Amazon, but I believe the long-term future of Amazon is in AWS. This segment is growing rapidly and showing incredible margin expansion. Commercial is a huge part of Amazon, of course, but the cloud is a real gem.

I do not exclude the possibility of a pullback in the stock price before the growth, and I view every pullback as an opportunity to add more, as long as the business of the company remains strong. In fact, I added more shares to my position early this week and plan to constantly increase this position, even though the valuation is a premium.

For me, personally, Amazon is a Strong Buy. And this is one of a few US tech companies I am ready to go into recession with.

Is the future AWS?

Retail remains the largest part of Amazon's revenue base, but AWS delivers the most profits. I've compared Net Sales (TTM) for Amazon in 2023 and 2024, you can see the comparison below in the picture. Commercial in North America drives the same 61% of the Net Sales for the company both in 2023 and 2024, but AWS continues to surprise investors in its own way.

In Q4 2024 alone, AWS generated $28.8 billion in revenue, growing 19% year-over-year, which is an incredible growth, but it can't justify the premium valuation of the stock of this company. What can, is its operating income. So, operating income in the trailing twelve months was $68.5 billion, up 86% year over year. An expansion this rapid in operating income is something unseen for a company of this size, and the reason for this expansion is AWS.

AWS is just 17% of total revenue, yet it generates more than half of Amazon's total operating income. Out of $68.5 billion, $39.8 billion was made from AWS. I must repeat it again: 17% of revenue drives 58% of profits. I am amazed by these numbers, although I am aware that still a huge part of the revenue comes from other segments, which we can't rule out, and Amazon is working hard to sustain those incomes.

AWS is such a big deal, and everyone talks about it because this isn't a one-time acceleration. This is a consistent trend, showing that AWS is becoming more profitable, more rooted, and more important to the tech industry quarter after quarter.

But numbers alone can't fully explain my point of view on Amazon. In general, I have a pretty personal approach to investing and choosing stocks. I tend to invest either in companies that I used and liked or the companies, business of which I find fascinating or unique (of course, I check the fundamentals as well).

Amazon and AWS are both. The majority of people in my surroundings are from the high-tech industry, and I can say that the demand for engineers who can integrate and manage AWS services has steadily grown over the past few years. If seven to eight years ago an AWS certificate was nice to have and some companies asked for it, now when looking for a job, "cloud experience" is a part of most requirements for the candidates, and when someone says "cloud" in high-tech, they mean AWS.

I recently checked U.S. job postings on Glassdoor and found around 41,800 software engineering positions open. Funny enough, the search for AWS engineer gave 22,700 positions. That's over half. AWS isn't just a desirable skill anymore - it's an industry standard, and the industry depends on it.

Backing this up further, research from AWSforEngineers.com shows that 76% of employers actively factor AWS certification into hiring decisions, and 60% prefer certified candidates. AWS-certified professionals are earning between $140K and $200K, and demand for this talent is expected to grow by 30% annually through 2025.

Getting an AWS certification can boost your pay. Studies show that AWS certified professionals often earn 25-30% more than those without certifications. This increase can change based on where you work, your industry, and how much experience you have.

This line of thought made me question the ability of AWS to progress. If everyone is using it already, what are the catalysts for growth? Besides the organic growth of the industry brought by the reliance of many businesses on AWS infrastructure, we must include the growth of the AI sector and everything related to it. Amazon is actively adding the number of solutions in areas like artificial intelligence and quantum computing (yes, actual commercial quantum computing through Amazon Braket), the use cases of which are only widening.

So, AWS not only captured a huge market share of modern companies, but it's already positioned itself to benefit and to profit from the next generation of innovation. Just this Thursday, Amazon CEO Andy Jassy reiterated that AI is one of the company's biggest priorities and investment areas. He made it clear that Amazon wants to be established in the middle of the AI boom, especially through AWS. Given the amazing reputation of the company and the strength of its branding, I have no reason to think AMZN won't be successful in capturing market share further on, including in the AI industry.

Commercial Still Matters, and It's at Risk

Retail is still Amazon's biggest segment, and I don't want to understate its importance. In Q4, 2024, Amazon made $75.5 billion from its online stores and another $47.5 billion from third-party seller services. These numbers are massive. This side of the business supports the whole Amazon ecosystem, including AWS, and thanks to this part of the business, the brand is one of the most well-known.

But it's also the segment most vulnerable right now.

If Trump continues escalating tariffs on China, as he just did, raising them to 125%, we could see a significant disruption in Amazon's retail supply chain. A huge portion of Amazon's retail products are supplied by Chinese vendors, and they're under pressure like never before. In fact, the head of China's largest e-commerce association told Reuters:

This isn't just a tax issue, it's that the entire cost structure gets entirely overwhelmed. It'll be very hard for anyone to survive in the U.S. market.
